title: The accounting system
summary: 9706 P2 — double entry, books of prime entry, ledgers, and the accounting cycle.
learningObjectives: Describe books of prime entry and the ledger.
Record transactions using debit and credit rules.
Outline flow from source documents to trial balance.

simpleExplanation.title: Every transaction has two sides
simpleExplanation.summary: Double entry records each transaction twice — debit and credit — so the accounting equation stays balanced. Day books collect transactions; the ledger holds T-accounts.
steps: Debit increases assets and expenses; credit increases liabilities and income.
Books of prime entry — sales day book, purchases day book, cash book.
Ledger — individual T-accounts; balances extracted for trial balance.
Source document → day book → ledger → trial balance → financial statements.

flashcards.back: Debit: Expenses, Assets, Drawings. Credit: Liabilities, Income, Capital.
Lists credit sales — total DR debtors control, CR sales.
Lists credit purchases — DR purchases, CR creditors control.
Records all bank/cash receipts and payments — also a ledger account.
Nominal accounts — expenses, income, assets, liabilities.
Receivables and payables — individual customer/supplier accounts.

sections.content: **The accounting system** (1.2.1) — master **DEAD CLIC** and **control account** postings before Paper 2 journal questions.

sections.question: Credit sale of goods $800 to Customer X. State the double-entry and which book of prime entry is used first.
sections.solution:
**Book of prime entry:** **Sales day book** — record **$800** to Customer X (listed, not double-entered in day book itself).

**Ledger entries (from total at period end or individually):**
- DR **Sales ledger control** (or Customer X) **$800**
- CR **Sales revenue** **$800**

**Effect:** **Asset (receivable)** up, **income** up — accounting equation balanced.
